text:
Large Language Models (LLMs) pre-trained on large codebases have recently shown impressive results on code intelligence tasks. While those models have a number of benefits, they suffer from two main drawbacks: (1) they are almost always limited to a fixed model architecture (such as encoder or decoder) which might not be the most efficient for each task, (2) they are only pre-trained on a fixed set of tasks (such as language modeling) which is not optimal since each task has its own specifics. To alleviate those issues, we introduce CodeT5+, a new family of encoder-decoder Large Language Models (LLMs) for code. CodeT5+ models are highly modular and their components can be easily swapped depending on the task at hand.

Through this work we show that, through carefully combining a wide range of pretraining objectives such as span denoising, contrastive learning, text-code matching and causal language modeling on unimodal and bimodal multilingual data, and leveraging the architectural flexibility provided by the T5 model, we can achieve strong performance on a large number of programming tasks. Training such models from scratch is computationally expensive and so to train CodeT5+ in a computationally efficient manner, we leverage pre-trained large language models (LLMs) in a freezing mode and perform instruction-tuning to match the intent of the human user. We conduct experiments across over 20 programming tasks in the zero-shot, few-shot, and instruction-tuning settings. Experimental results show that CodeT5+ achieves state-of-the-art results on almost all the benchmark tasks. Moreover, our instruction-tuned CodeT5+ 16B model reaches a new state-of-the-art on the HumanEval code generation benchmark with a pass@1 of 35.0% and pass@10 of 54.5%, surpassing all current open-source code LLMs, as well as the OpenAI’s code-cushman-001 model.
explanation: The text reads like a polished research abstract with detailed model terminology and plausible results, which could be human-written; there are no strong red flags of AI authorship.
